description: Complete guide to singly linked lists: node structure, insertion (beginning, end, position), deletion, traversal, search, reversal, and complexity analysis. Many examples for beginners.

sections.title: What is a Singly Linked List?
sections.definition: A singly linked list is a linear data structure where each element (node) contains data and a pointer to the next node. It allows dynamic memory allocation and efficient insertions/deletions compared to arrays.
sections.analogy: Like a treasure hunt where each clue tells you where the next clue is. You can only go forward, not backward.

sections.title: Node Structure
sections.definition: Each node contains data (any type) and a pointer to the next node. The last node points to nullptr.

sections.title: Creating and Traversing a List
sections.definition: Start with head pointer (nullptr for empty list). Traverse by following next pointers until nullptr.

sections.title: Insertion at Beginning
sections.definition: Create new node, set its next to current head, then update head to point to new node. O(1) time.

sections.title: Insertion at End
sections.definition: Traverse to last node, set its next to new node. If list empty, make head point to new node. O(n) time.

sections.title: Insertion at a Given Position (0-indexed)
sections.definition: Insert before position i. Handle beginning (i==0) and middle/end. O(n) time.

sections.title: Deletion by Value
sections.definition: Find node with given value, adjust previous node's next pointer to skip it. Delete node. O(n).

sections.title: Deletion at Position
sections.definition: Remove node at given index. O(n).

sections.title: Searching for an Element
sections.definition: Traverse and compare data. Return true if found. O(n).

sections.title: Reversing a Singly Linked List
sections.definition: Iterative method: use three pointers (prev, curr, next) to reverse links. O(n) time, O(1) space.

sections.title: Finding Middle of Linked List (Slow-Fast Pointer)
sections.definition: Use two pointers: slow moves one step, fast moves two steps. When fast reaches end, slow is at middle. O(n).

sections.title: Detecting Cycle (Floyd's Cycle Detection)
sections.definition: Use slow and fast pointers. If they meet, cycle exists. O(n) time.

sections.title: Memory Management and Destructor
sections.definition: Always delete nodes to avoid memory leaks. A destructor can traverse and delete all nodes.
